summary: Rory is a 26-29 year old medical student who has created a highly systematized approach to food that prioritizes efficiency over enjoyment. Having transitioned from dentistry to medicine, he operates with the pragmatic mindset of someone managing multiple demands - full-time medical placement, part-time dental work, and business interests. His food strategy is built around outsourcing: meal prep deliveries for weekday dinners, repetitive lunch preparation, and strategic use of delivery apps when his systems break down. He approaches food decisions with the same analytical framework he applies to everything else, from loyalty programs to subscription services. What's striking is his complete separation of cooking from daily life - it exists only as a social activity for entertaining others, never for personal nourishment or pleasure. His relationship with delivery apps mirrors his broader approach to life: highly optimized, loyalty-driven when it serves his efficiency goals, but ultimately transactional rather than emotional.
personality_notes: Analytical and efficiency-focused communicator who gives detailed, structured responses. Approaches decisions systematically and shows strong optimization mindset. Pragmatic rather than emotional in his explanations.
food_relationship: Food is purely functional - a problem to be solved through systematic outsourcing rather than a source of pleasure or creativity.

key_moments.description: Describes cooking as 'almost never' except when entertaining others
key_moments.significance: Reveals complete separation between food as fuel vs food as social activity

key_moments.description: Admits to 'terrible habit' of eating same lunch for months until sick of it
key_moments.significance: Shows tension between claimed lack of food stress and actual problematic patterns

key_moments.description: Explains comprehensive Uber ecosystem loyalty strategy
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates how he systematizes all life decisions, including food delivery

what_not_said: Any emotional connection to food or cooking
initial_hypotheses: Food decisions are driven entirely by efficiency optimization rather than taste or health
His systematic approach may mask underlying food relationship issues or avoidance
Delivery apps succeed with him when they integrate into broader life systems rather than standalone food solutions
His 'terrible habit' comment suggests some awareness that his food patterns aren't ideal but no motivation to change
questions_for_coding: How do highly systematic users like Rory evaluate food delivery platforms differently?
What role does ecosystem integration play in platform loyalty for efficiency-focused users?
How do users rationalize problematic food patterns while claiming no stress?
What triggers cause systematic food approaches to break down and require delivery?
